startScreenOptions.title: AN13.1-AN13.8 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for AN13.1-AN13.8.

interactions.label: Which superficial vein of the upper limb pierces the deep fascia at mid-arm and

params.question: Which superficial vein of the upper limb pierces the deep fascia at mid-arm and joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The cephalic vein does not pierce the deep fascia at mid-arm. It travels superficially up the lateral arm and passes through the deltopectoral groove to drain into the axillary vein.

Correct. The basilic vein runs up the medial forearm, pierces the deep fascia at mid-arm, and joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein. This deeper course makes it less suitable for venepuncture than the cephalic or median cubital vein.
The median cubital vein is a connecting vein between the cephalic and basilic veins at the cubital fossa. It does not travel up the arm or pierce the deep fascia.
The median antebrachial vein runs superficially along the middle of the anterior forearm and usually drains into the median cubital vein. It does not pierce the deep fascia at mid-arm.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the two main superficial veins. The cephalic vein stays superficial and joins the axillary vein at the deltopectoral groove. The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia at mid-arm and joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.

Well done. The basilic vein's course — piercing the deep fascia at mid-arm — distinguishes it from the cephalic vein, which remains superficial throughout and enters the axillary vein via the deltopectoral groove.

metadata.title: Which superficial vein of the upper limb

interactions.label: The annular ligament encircles the head of the ulna, allowing pronation and supi

params.question: The annular ligament encircles the head of the ulna, allowing pronation and supination at the proximal radio-ulnar joint.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The statement is actually false. The annular ligament encircles the head of the radius, not the ulna. The radius is the bone that rotates during pronation and supination, and the annular ligament holds it in place within the radial notch of the ulna. This distinction is clinically important — in young children, a pulled elbow occurs when the radial head slips out of this ring.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. The annular ligament encircles the head of the radius, not the ulna. It holds the radial head in the radial notch of the ulna like a collar, permitting rotation during pronation and supination. In young children, a sudden pull on the arm can slip the radial head partially out of this ring, causing a pulled elbow (nursemaid's elbow).

interactions.label: When a hand must be immobilised, the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints should be

params.question: When a hand must be immobilised, the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints should be splinted at 70–90 degrees of flexion. What is the anatomical reason for this 'position of safety'?

answers.text: It maximises blood flow to the digits by opening the palmar arterial arches

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The palmar arterial arches supply the digits regardless of MCP joint position. The position of safety is determined by ligament mechanics, not vascular anatomy.

answers.text: It keeps the collateral ligaments taut, preventing them from shortening into a permanent contracture

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The collateral ligaments of the MCP joints are slack in extension but become taut in flexion. Splinting in flexion maintains their stretched length. If immobilised in extension, these ligaments shorten and form a contracture that permanently restricts flexion.

answers.text: It reduces swelling by placing the hand above heart level

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Elevation does reduce swelling, but it refers to the position of the whole limb, not the angle of the MCP joints. The position of safety specifically addresses collateral ligament mechanics.

answers.text: It relaxes the long flexor tendons to prevent adhesion formation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tendon adhesion is a valid clinical concern after hand injury, but the position of safety is determined by the behaviour of the MCP collateral ligaments, which are taut in flexion and slack in extension.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key concept is that the MCP collateral ligaments become taut in flexion and are slack in extension. If the hand is immobilised in extension, these slack ligaments shorten and form a contracture. Splinting in 70–90 degrees of flexion keeps them stretched and prevents this complication.

Excellent. The MCP collateral ligaments are taut in flexion and slack in extension. Splinting at 70–90 degrees of flexion prevents these ligaments from shortening, preserving the patient's ability to make a full fist after recovery.

interactions.label: The medial end of the spine of the scapula lies at the level of the seventh thor

params.question: The medial end of the spine of the scapula lies at the level of the seventh thoracic vertebral spine (T7).

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is false. The medial end of the spine of the scapula lies at T3, not T7. You may be confusing it with the inferior angle of the scapula, which lies at T7. Remember the pair: scapular spine = T3, inferior angle = T7.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. The medial end of the spine of the scapula lies at the level of T3, not T7. It is the inferior angle of the scapula that lies at T7. A useful way to remember: the spine is higher (T3) and the tip is lower (T7).

metadata.title: The medial end of the spine of the scapu

interactions.label: During a clinical examination, you ask a patient to push against a wall with out

params.question: During a clinical examination, you ask a patient to push against a wall with outstretched arms and observe that the medial border of the scapula lifts away from the chest wall. Which nerve is most likely damaged?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid muscle. Damage causes loss of the rounded shoulder contour and weakness of abduction, not winging of the scapula.

Correct. The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior, which protracts the scapula and holds it flat against the chest wall. When this nerve is damaged, the scapula 'wings' — its medial border lifts away, especially during forward pushing movements.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi, which extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm. Damage weakens these movements but does not cause winging of the scapula.

The spinal accessory nerve (cranial nerve XI) supplies the trapezius. Damage causes difficulty shrugging the shoulder and weakness of scapular retraction, but the characteristic sign is shoulder droop, not winging.

overallFeedback.feedback: Winging of the scapula — the medial border lifting off the chest wall — is caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle, which is supplied by the long thoracic nerve. Each motor nerve to the shoulder region produces a distinct clinical sign when damaged.

Well done. Winging of the scapula is the hallmark sign of long thoracic nerve injury. The serratus anterior normally anchors the scapula to the chest wall; without it, the medial border lifts away during forward pushing.

interactions.label: Which signalling molecule, produced by the zone of polarising activity (ZPA), de

params.question: Which signalling molecule, produced by the zone of polarising activity (ZPA), determines the identity and number of digits — and when produced in excess, causes polydactyly?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: FGF is released by the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) and drives proximal-to-distal limb outgrowth. It does not determine digit identity or number.
BMP is involved in dorsal-ventral patterning of the limb, working alongside Wnt signalling. It does not determine digit number or identity.

Correct. Sonic Hedgehog protein is produced by the ZPA and controls anterior-to-posterior patterning — determining which digit is thumb, index, and so on. Excess SHH leads to extra digits (polydactyly).
Wnt signalling is involved in determining dorsal-ventral identity of the limb (e.g., distinguishing the back of the hand from the palm). It does not control digit number.

overallFeedback.feedback: Each signalling centre controls a different axis of limb development. The ZPA produces Sonic Hedgehog for anterior-posterior patterning (digit identity and number). The AER produces FGF for proximal-distal outgrowth. Wnt and BMP control dorsal-ventral identity.

Excellent. Sonic Hedgehog from the ZPA controls the anterior-posterior axis — digit identity and number. Remember the three centres: AER (proximal-distal via FGF), ZPA (anterior-posterior via SHH), and Wnt/BMP (dorsal-ventral).
